question: Does the generated text correctly associate Daylight with a bluish cast (high color temperature), Fluorescent with a greenish cast (spectral spike), and Tungsten with an orange-yellow cast (low color temperature)?
Does the text cite appropriate approximate Kelvin values for the light sources (e.g., Tungsten ~2700-3200K, Daylight ~5500-6500K)?
Does the generated image representing 'Daylight' clearly depict a white shirt with a visible bluish-white tint?
Does the generated image representing 'Fluorescent light' clearly depict a white shirt with a visible greenish tint?
Does the generated image representing 'Tungsten light' clearly depict a white shirt with a visible orange or yellow tint?
Does the generated 'color temperature-color cast coordinate diagram' visually represent two distinct axes: Color Temperature (Blue-Amber/Yellow) and Tint (Green-Magenta)?
On the coordinate diagram, is the Blue direction correctly associated with higher Kelvin values (Cool) and the Amber/Yellow direction with lower Kelvin values (Warm)?
Are the text labels and axis markings within the generated coordinate diagram legible and free from severe artifacting?
Do the three comparison images maintain a consistent visual style, subject matter (same shirt), and composition, with only the lighting cast varying?
Is each comparison image placed immediately adjacent to or following the text paragraph describing that specific light source?
Did the model generate exactly four distinct visual elements as requested: three specific comparison images (Daylight, Fluorescent, Tungsten) and one coordinate diagram?

Does the output provide a clear explanation of which direction to adjust color temperature and tint for post-processing correction for each of the three lighting scenarios (e.g., warming up daylight shots, adding magenta for fluorescent)?

Does the explanation correctly distinguish between adjusting Color Temperature (Blue-Yellow axis) and Tint (Green-Magenta axis), specifically noting that fluorescent correction requires Tint adjustment?
